docs.text:
Banshee is a command-line tool for fast, efficient access to Recorded Future Intelligence, built for security professionals and SOC teams.

Powered by PSEngine

Banshee is powered by the PSEngine library.

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text:
• IOC lookup and search
  • Packet capture (pcap) analysis
  • Classic and Playbook Alert management
  • List & Watch List management
  • Entity search and lookup

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
Banshee is distributed as a Python package. To install, run:

pip install banshee-0.0.3-py3-none-any.whl

Banshee officially supports Python 3.9 and above.

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text:
All required Python dependencies are resolved automatically by pip. To use the pcap command, ensure you have:

  • tshark 4.4.5 or later

docs.title: Command Auto Completion
docs.text:
After installing Banshee, enable command auto completion with:

banshee --install-completion

Restart your shell to complete the installation. You can now use TAB to auto-complete commands.

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text:
To view available commands, just run:

banshee

docs.title: Authorization
docs.text:
Provide your Recorded Future API key using the -k or --api-key argument, or set it as the RF_TOKEN environment variable:

banshee -k <RF_TOKEN> <command> <sub-command> <arguments>

docs.title: Proxies
docs.text:
If you are behind a proxy, set the HTTP_PROXY and HTTPS_PROXY environment variables.

To disable SSL verification, use the -s flag:

banshee -s ca rules

docs.title: Next steps
docs.text: Get started using banshee now!

docs.title: Added
docs.text:
• New ca update sub-command to update one or more Classic Alerts.
  • New pba update sub-command to update one or more Playbook Alerts.
  • pba commands now support two new categories: malware_report and geopolitics_facility.
  • Python 3.13 compatibility.
  • tshark version check now enforces minimum version 4.4.5.

docs.title: Fixed
docs.text:
• pcap analyze no longer crashes due to version mismatch.
  • Improved exception handling throughout the CLI.

docs.title: Changed
docs.text:
• ioc search ENTITY_TYPE IOC now accepts whitespace separatated IOCs, instead of a comma-separated string.
  • pba lookup ALERT_ID -p output formatting improved.
  • ca search --triggered now supports time ranges.
  • ca search -r now accepts multiple rules by repeating -r (e.g. -r rule1 -r rule2), instead of a comma-separated string.
  • Upgraded PSEngine to v2.0.6.

docs.title: Added
docs.text:
• New entity command to search and lookup entities
  • New list command to manage Recorded Future Lists & Watch Lists
  • New ioc rules sub-command to search and filter IOC rules
  • New --debug option for enchanced troubleshooting

docs.title: Changed
docs.text:
• Sub-command ioc lookup option -v now allows the user to pick a level of verbosity (from 1 to 5)
  • Sub-command ioc lookup now requires an entity type as an argument, for example banshee ioc lookup ip 8.8.8.8
  • Sub-command ca lookup <alert id< -p returns a refined pretty alert
  • PSEngine upgraded to v2.0.2

docs.title: Added
docs.text:
• Beta release

🚀 Brought to you by the Cyber Security Engineers at Recorded

docs.title: About
docs.text: Banshee is a command-line interface (CLI) tool developed and maintained by the Recorded Future Professional Services Cyber Security Engineering team. This project is designed to provide security professionals with quick and efficient access to Recorded Future Intelligence through a comprehensive set of terminal-based commands.

docs.title: Project Support
docs.text: Banshee is officially supported by Recorded Future's Professional Services team. Our cyber security engineers have built this tool to streamline your security operations and investigations.

docs.title: Getting Help
docs.text:
• Account Questions: For questions about your Recorded Future account, licensing, or to learn more about our Professional Services offerings, please reach out to your account representative at Recorded Future.

  • Technical Support: For technical support, bug reports, or general assistance with Banshee, please contact us at support@recordedfuture.com.

docs.title: Professional Services
docs.text:
The Recorded Future Professional Services team offers comprehensive cybersecurity consulting, implementation support, and custom development services. If you're interested in learning more about how our team can help enhance your security operations, please contact your account representative.

🚀 Brought to you by the Cyber Security Engineers at Recorded Future

docs.title: Getting started
docs.text:
To help you get started with banshee, we'll cover a few important topics:

  • Installing banshee
  • First steps after installation
  • How to get help

docs.title: First steps with banshee
docs.text:
After installing banshee, you can check that banshee is available by running the banshee command:

You should see a help menu listing the available commands.

docs.title: Authorization
docs.text:
Provide your Recorded Future API key using the -k or --api-key argument, or set it as the RF_TOKEN environment variable:

banshee -k <RF_TOKEN> <command> <sub-command> <arguments>

docs.title: Proxies
docs.text:
If you are behind a proxy, set the HTTP_PROXY and HTTPS_PROXY environment variables.

To disable SSL verification, use the -s flag:

banshee -s ca rules

docs.title: Next steps
docs.text: Now that you've confirmed banshee is installed jump to the commands reference to start using banshee, and learn how to get help if you run into any issues.

docs.title: Help menus
docs.text:
The --help, -h flag can be used to view the help menu for a command, e.g., for banshee:

$ banshee --help

To view the help menu for a specific command, e.g., for banshee pcap:

$ banshee pcap --help

docs.title: Viewing the version
docs.text:
When seeking help, it's important to determine the version of banshee that you're using — sometimes the problem is already solved in a newer version.

To check the installed version:

$ banshee --version

docs.title: Errors
docs.text:
To enhance the error if a command is failing in unexpected ways, the --debug flag can be used:

banshee --debug ioc search ip -p

The output will display where exactly banshee is failing over. This information can then be passed to our support team to aid you in troubleshooting.

docs.title: Open a support case with Recorded Future Support
docs.text: Submit a support request for help alternativelly reach out to support@recordedfuture.com.

docs.title: Installation methods
docs.text: Install banshee with pip.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
Banshee is distributed as a Python package. To install, run:

pip install banshee-0.0.3-py3-none-any.whl

Tip

Banshee officially supports Python 3.9 and above.

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text:
All required Python dependencies are resolved automatically by pip. To use the pcap command, ensure you have:

  • tshark 4.4.5 or later

docs.title: Upgrading banshee
docs.text:
Upgrading is as easy as rerunning the installation with the new version wheel file:

$ pip install banshee-<new-version>-py3-none-any.whl

docs.title: Shell autocompletion
docs.text:
After installing Banshee, enable command auto completion with:

banshee --install-completion

Restart your shell to complete the installation. You can now use TAB to auto-complete commands.

docs.title: Uninstallation
docs.text:
If you need to remove banshee from your system, run:

$ pip uninstall banshee

docs.title: Next steps
docs.text: See the first steps to start using banshee.

docs.title: Reference
docs.text:
The reference section provides information about specific parts of banshee:

  • Commands: A reference for banshee's command line interface.
  • Environment Variables: A reference for banshee's environment variables.

docs.title: banshee
docs.text:
Banshee is a command-line tool for fast, efficient access to Recorded Future Intelligence, built for security professionals and SOC teams.

Usage 
banshee [OPTIONS] <COMMAND>
Commands banshee ca

Search, lookup and update Recorded Future Classic Alerts

banshee entity

Search and lookup Recorded Future entities

banshee ioc

Search and lookup Indicators of Compromise (IOCs)

banshee list

Manage Recorded Future lists and Watch lists

banshee pba

Search, lookup and update Recorded Future Playbook Alerts

banshee pcap

Analyze packet capture (pcap) files by enriching them with Recorded Future Intelligence

docs.title: banshee ca
docs.text:
Search, lookup and update Recorded Future Classic Alerts

Usage 
banshee ca [OPTIONS] COMMAND [ARGS]...
Commands banshee ca lookup

Lookup a Classic Alert

banshee ca search

Search for Classic Alerts

banshee ca rules

Search for Classic Alert rules

banshee ca update

Update one or more Classic Alert

docs.title: banshee ca lookupUsageArgumentsOptions
docs.text:
Lookup a Classic Alert.

By default the command will print the results in JSON format.

banshee ca lookup [OPTIONS] ALERT_ID
ALER_ID

Alert ID to lookup

--pretty, -p

Pretty print the results in a human readable format

--help, -h

Show help for this command

docs.title: banshee ca searchUsageOptions
docs.text:
Search for Classic Alerts.

By default the command will print the results in JSON format.

banshee ca search [OPTIONS]
--triggered, -t triggered

Filter on triggered time, for example: 1d; 12h; [2024-08-01, 2024-08-14]; [2024-09-23 12:03:58.000, 2024-09-23 12:03:58.567)

Defaults to 1d

--rule rule-name

Filter by an alert rule name (freetext)

--pretty, -p

Pretty print the results in a human readable format

--help, -h

Show help for this command

docs.title: banshee ca rulesUsageOptions
docs.text:
Search for Classic Alert rules.

By default the command will print the results in JSON format.

banshee ca rules [OPTIONS] [FREETEXT]
--pretty, -p

Pretty print the results in a human readable format

--help, -h

Show help for this command

docs.title: banshee ca updateUsageArgumentsOptionsExample UsageSupplying Alert IDsNote Append
docs.text:
Update one or more Classic Alert

banshee ca update [OPTIONS] ALERT_IDS...
ALERT_IDS

One or more whitespace separated Alert ID

--status, -s alert-status

Update the alert(s) to this alert status

Possible values are: New, Pending, Dismissed, Resolved

--note, -n note

Note text for the alert.

The length limit for the note is 1000 characters

--append, -a

This flag will append the note text if the alert aleady has a note

--assignee, -a assignee

New user to assign the alert(s) to. Accepts uhash or email address of the user, for example: uhash:3aXZxdkM12, analyst@acme.com

--help, -h

Show help for this command

Provide one or more Alert IDs (whitespace separated) and specify the desired update options:


banshee ca update  -s Dismissed
banshee ca update  -s Dismissed -n "note text"
banshee ca update  -s Dismissed -n "note text" -a analyst@acme.com




1. Directly as arguments (single or multiple):



banshee ca update ALERT_ID -s Resolved
banshee ca update ALERT_ID_1 ALERT_ID_2 -s Pending



2. From a file or standard input:


If you have a file (e.g., alerts.txt) with one Alert ID per line:




ALERT_ID_1
ALERT_ID_2
ALERT_ID_3




You can update all listed alerts using:




banshee ca update -s Dismissed < alerts.txt
cat alerts.txt | banshee ca update -s Dismissed



3. By piping from a search command:


Use tools like jq to extract Alert IDs from search results and pipe them into the update command:




banshee ca search | jq -r '.[].id' | banshee ca update -n "Investigation started"






Classic Alerts support only a single note. By default, the update command will overwrite the existing note with the new one.
If you wish to append a new note instead, use the --append (-A) option.

docs.title: banshee entity

docs.title: banshee ioc
docs.text:
Search and lookup Indicators of Compromise (IOCs)

Usage 
banshee ioc [OPTIONS] COMMAND [ARGS]...
Commands banshee ioc lookup

Lookup one or more IOCs

banshee ioc search

Search for IOCs

banshee ioc rules

Search for IOC rules

docs.title: banshee ioc lookupUsageArgumentsOptionsExample Usage
docs.text:
Lookup one or more IOCs.

By default the command will print the results in JSON format.

banshee ioc lookup [OPTIONS] ENTITY_TYPE IOC...
ENTITY_TYPE

Entity type to lookup

Supported values: ip, domain, url, hash, vulnerability

IOC

One or more whitespace separated IOC to lookup

--verbosity, -v verbosity-level

Verbosity level (1 to 5) of the output (higher verbosity more data is returned)

Defaults to 1

Verbosity Levels

ip:

  • 1: entity, risk, timestamps
  • 2: counts, enterpriseLists, entity, metrics, risk, threatLists, timestamps
  • 3: analystNotes, counts, enterpriseLists, entity, intelCard, links, metrics, relatedEntities, risk, threatLists, timestamps
  • 4: analystNotes, counts, enterpriseLists, entity, intelCard, links, location, metrics, relatedEntities, risk, riskMapping, sightings, threatLists, timestamps
  • 5: aiInsights, analystNotes, counts, dnsPortCert, enterpriseLists, entity, intelCard, links, location, metrics, relatedEntities, risk, riskMapping, sightings, threatLists, timestamps

domain:

  • 1: entity, risk, timestamps
  • 2: counts, entity, metrics, risk, timestamps
  • 3: analystNotes, counts, entity, intelCard, links, metrics, relatedEntities, risk, timestamps
  • 4: analystNotes, counts, entity, intelCard, links, metrics, relatedEntities, risk, riskMapping, sightings, timestamps
  • 5: aiInsights, analystNotes, counts, entity, intelCard, links, metrics, relatedEntities, risk, riskMapping, sightings, timestamps

url:

  • 1: entity, risk, timestamps
  • 2: counts, entity, metrics, risk, timestamps
  • 3: analystNotes, counts, entity, intelCard, links, metrics, relatedEntities, risk, timestamps
  • 4: analystNotes, counts, entity, intelCard, links, metrics, relatedEntities, risk, riskMapping, sightings, timestamps
  • 5: aiInsights, analystNotes, counts, entity, intelCard, links, metrics, relatedEntities, risk, riskMapping, sightings, timestamps

hash:

  • 1: entity, risk, timestamps
  • 2: counts, entity, metrics, risk, timestamps
  • 3: analystNotes, counts, entity, fileHashes, hashAlgorithm, intelCard, links, metrics, relatedEntities, risk, timestamps
  • 4: analystNotes, counts, entity, fileHashes, hashAlgorithm, intelCard, links, metrics, relatedEntities, risk, riskMapping, sightings, timestamps
  • 5: aiInsights, analystNotes, counts, entity, fileHashes, hashAlgorithm, intelCard, links, metrics, relatedEntities, risk, riskMapping, sightings, timestamps

vulnerability:

  • 1: entity, risk, timestamps
  • 2: counts, entity, metrics, risk, timestamps
  • 3: analystNotes, counts, entity, intelCard, links, metrics, relatedEntities, risk, timestamps
  • 4: analystNotes, counts, entity, intelCard, links, metrics, relatedEntities, risk, riskMapping, sightings, timestamps
  • 5: aiInsights, analystNotes, counts, entity, intelCard, links, metrics, relatedEntities, risk, riskMapping, sightings, timestamps
--pretty, -p

Pretty print the results in a human readable format

--help, -h

Show help for this command


banshee ioc lookup ip 139.224.189.177
banshee ioc lookup domain overafazg.org
banshee ioc lookup ip 8.140.135.23 -v 3
banshee ioc lookup ip 8.140.135.23,139.224.189.177 -p

Pipe a comma or newline separated list of IOCs to lookup:


cat test_ips.csv| banshee ioc lookup ip -p

docs.title: banshee ioc searchUsageArgumentsOptions
docs.text:
Search for Classic Alerts.

By default the command will print the results in JSON format.

banshee ioc search [OPTIONS] ENTITY_TYPE
ENTITY_TYPE

Entity type to lookup

Supported values: ip, domain, url, hash, vulnerability

--limit, -l limit

Limit the number of results

The maximum limit is 1000

Defaults to 5

--risk-score, -r risk-score

Filter by risk score range, for example:

  • --risk-score '[20,90]' → same as 20 <= riskScore <= 90
  • --risk-score '(20,90)' → same as 20 < riskScore < 90
  • --risk-score '[20,90)' → same as 20 <= riskScore < 90
  • --risk-score '[20,)' → same as 20 <= riskScore
  • --risk-score '[,90)' → same as riskScore < 90

Surround the risk score range with quotes to ensure correct parsing

--risk-rule, -R rule-name

Filter by risk rule name

For available options refer to this support article, specifically the Machine Name column in the risk rules tables, or use the banshee ioc rules command

--pretty, -p

Pretty print the results in a human readable format

--help, -h

Show help for this command

docs.title: banshee ioc rulesUsageArgumentsOptions
docs.text:
Search for IOC rules for a given entity type.

By default the command will print the results in JSON format.

banshee ioc rules [OPTIONS] ENTITY_TYPE
ENTITY_TYPE

Entity type of the IOC rules

Supported values: ip, domain, url, hash, vulnerability

--freetext, -F freetext-rule-name

Filter by risk rule name using freetext search

--mitre-code, -M mitre-code

Filter by MITRE ATT&CK code

--criticality, -C criticality

Filter by criticality. Higher the value, higher the criticality

Accepted values are from 1 to 5

Criticality Levels (IP, Domain, URL, Hash)

  • 4 – Very Malicious (Risk Score band: 90–99)
  • 3 – Malicious (Risk Score band: 65–89)
  • 2 – Suspicious (Risk Score band: 25–64)
  • 1 – Unusual (Risk Score band: 5–24)
  • 0 – No evidence of risk (Risk Score band: 0)

Criticality Levels (Vulnerability)

  • 5 – Very Critical (Risk Score band: 90–99)
  • 4 – Critical (Risk Score band: 80–89)
  • 3 – High (Risk Score band: 65–79)
  • 2 – Medium (Risk Score band: 25–64)
  • 1 – Low (Risk Score band: 5–24)
  • 0 – No evidence of risk (Risk Score band: 0)
--pretty, -p

Pretty print the results in a human readable format

--help, -h

Show help for this command

docs.title: banshee list
docs.text:
Manage Recorded Future lists and Watch lists

Usage 
banshee list [OPTIONS] COMMAND [ARGS]...
banshee list create

Create a new list

banshee list info

Get basic information about a list

banshee list search

Search for lists

banshee list status

Get the status of a list

banshee list entities

Get the entities in a list

banshee list add

Add an entity to a list

banshee list bulk-add

Bulk add entities to a list

banshee list remove

Remove an entity from a list

banshee list bulk-remove

Bulk remove entities from a list

banshee list clear

Clear all entities from a list

banshee list entries

Get text entries from a list

docs.title: banshee list createUsageArgumentsOptions
docs.text:
Create a new list.

By default the command will print the results in JSON format.

banshee list create [OPTIONS] NAME [LIST_TYPE]
NAME

List name to create

LIST_TYPE

List type to create

Supported types are:

  • entity
  • source
  • text

Defaults to entity

--pretty, -p

Pretty print the results in a human readable format

--help, -h

Show help for this command

docs.title: banshee list infoUsageArgumentsOptions
docs.text:
Get information about a list, such as name, type, timestamps and owner details.

By default the command will print the results in JSON format.

banshee list info [OPTIONS] LIST_ID
LIST_ID

List ID to get information about

List ID can be supplied with and without the 'report:' prefix

--pretty, -p

Pretty print the results in a human readable format

--help, -h

Show help for this command

docs.title: banshee list searchUsageArgumentsOptions
docs.text:
Search for lists

By default the command will print the results in JSON format.

banshee list search [OPTIONS] LIST_ID

List name to search for

Not specifying a name will return all lists

--list-type, -t list-type

Filter by list type

Supported types:

  • entity
  • source
  • text
  • custom
  • ip
  • domain
  • tech_stack
  • industry
  • brand
  • partner
  • industry_peer
  • location
  • supplier
  • vulnerability
  • company
  • hash
  • operation
  • attacker
  • target
  • method
  • executive

--limit, -l limit

Limit the number of results

Defaults to 10

--pretty, -p

Pretty print the results in a human readable format

--help, -h

Show help for this command

LIST_ID

List ID can be supplied with and without the 'report:' prefix

LIST_ID

List ID can be supplied with and without the 'report:' prefix

Get text entries on the list

By default the command will print the results in JSON format.

banshee list entries [OPTIONS] LIST_ID
LIST_ID

List ID to fetch text entries from

List ID can be supplied with and without the 'report:' prefix

docs.title: banshee list clearUsageArgumentsOptions
docs.text:
Fully clear the list and remove all entities. Please note this command will not clear text entries and is not supported.

banshee list clear [OPTIONS] LIST_ID
LIST_ID

List ID to clear

List ID can be supplied with and without the 'report:' prefix

--help, -h

Show help for this command

docs.title: banshee list addUsageArgumentsOptions
docs.text:
Add an entity to the list.

banshee list add [OPTIONS] LIST_ID ENTITY_ID [PROPERTIES]
LIST_ID

List ID to add to

List ID can be supplied with and without the 'report:' prefix

ENTITY_ID

Entity ID or name with type to add to the list, for example:

  • SoA6SP
  • wannacry,malware
  • www.duckdns.org,InternetDomainName
PROPERTIES

Optional properties to set on the entity

Properties can be supplied as key=value pairs, for example: type=malware,cool=beans,another=value

--help, -h

Show help for this command

docs.title: banshee list bulk-addUsageArgumentsOptions
docs.text:
Add multiple entities to a list

banshee list bulk-add [OPTIONS] LIST_ID ENTITY_INPUT...
LIST_ID

List ID to add to

List ID can be supplied with and without the 'report:' prefix

ENTITY_ID

One or more space/newline separated entities, for example:

  • SoA6SP
  • wannacry,malware
  • www.duckdns.org,InternetDomainName

The command also accepts input from stdin. Assume 'entities.txt' is a newline separated file of entities, for example:


    $ cat entities.txt
    verifyaccount.otzo.com,InternetDomainName
    92.38.178.133,IpAddress
    https://constructorachg.cl/eFSLb6eV/j.html,URL
    CVE-2019-1215,CyberVulnerability
    e3f236e4aeb73f8f8f0caebe46f53abbb2f71fa4b266a34ab50e01933709e877,Hash
    SoA6SP
    lYNvCK

Considering the above you could then run one of the following commands to bulk add the entities:


    $ banshee list bulk-add LIST_ID < entities.txt
    $ cat entities.txt | banshee list bulk-add LIST_ID
--help, -h

Show help for this command

docs.title: banshee list removeUsageArgumentsOptions
docs.text:
Remove an entity from the list.

banshee list remove [OPTIONS] LIST_ID ENTITY_ID
LIST_ID

List ID to remove from

List ID can be supplied with and without the 'report:' prefix

ENTITY_ID

Entity ID to remove from the list

--help, -h

Show help for this command

docs.title: banshee list bulk-removeUsageArgumentsOptions
docs.text:
Remove multiple entities from a list

banshee list bulk-remove [OPTIONS] LIST_ID ENTITY_INPUT...
LIST_ID

List ID to remove from

List ID can be supplied with and without the 'report:' prefix

ENTITY_ID

One or more space/newline separated entities, for example:

  • SoA6SP
  • wannacry,malware
  • www.duckdns.org,InternetDomainName

The command also accepts input from stdin. Assume 'entities.txt' is a newline separated file of entities, for example:


    $ cat entities.txt
    verifyaccount.otzo.com,InternetDomainName
    92.38.178.133,IpAddress
    https://constructorachg.cl/eFSLb6eV/j.html,URL
    CVE-2019-1215,CyberVulnerability
    e3f236e4aeb73f8f8f0caebe46f53abbb2f71fa4b266a34ab50e01933709e877,Hash
    SoA6SP
    lYNvCK

Considering the above you could then run one of the following commands to bulk remove the entities:


    $ banshee list bulk-remove LIST_ID < entities.txt
    $ cat entities.txt | banshee list bulk-remove LIST_ID
--help, -h

Show help for this command

docs.title: banshee pba
docs.text:
Search, lookup and update Recorded Future Playbook Alerts

Usage 
banshee pba [OPTIONS] COMMAND [ARGS]...
Commands banshee pba lookup

Lookup a Playook Alert

banshee pba search

Search for Playook Alerts

banshee pba update

Update one or more Playook Alert

docs.title: banshee pba lookupUsageArgumentsOptions
docs.text:
Lookup a Playook Alert.

By default the command will print the results in JSON format.

banshee pba lookup [OPTIONS] ALERT_ID
ALER_ID

Alert ID to lookup

Alert ID can be supplied with and without the 'task:' prefix

--pretty, -p

Pretty print the results in a human readable format

--help, -h

Show help for this command

docs.title: banshee pba searchUsageOptions
docs.text:
Search for Playook Alerts.

By default the command will print the results in JSON format.

banshee pba search [OPTIONS]
--created, -C created-from

Filter on the created from time, for example: 1d; 12h

--updated, -u updated-from

Filter on the updated from time, for example: 1d; 12h

--category, -c category

Filter by alert category

Supported categories:

  • domain_abuse
  • cyber_vulnerability
  • third_party_risk
  • code_repo_leakage
  • identity_novel_exposures
  • geopolitics_facility

--priority, -P priority

Filter by alert priority

Possible values are: Informational, Moderate, High

Defaults to all categories

--status, -s alert-status

Filter by alert status

Possible values are: New, InProgress, Dismissed, Resolved

Defaults to all categories

--limit, -l limit

Limit the number of results

The maximum limit is 10 000

Defaults to 10

--pretty, -p

Pretty print the results in a human readable format

--help, -h

Show help for this command

docs.title: banshee pba updateUsageArgumentsOptionsExample UsageSupplying Alert IDsAdditional Usage Examples
docs.text:
Update one or more Playook Alert

banshee pba update [OPTIONS] ALERT_IDS...
ALERT_IDS

One or more whitespace separated Alert ID

Alert ID can be supplied with and without the 'task:' prefix

--status, -s alert-status

Update the alert(s) to this alert status

Possible values are: New, InProgress, Dismissed, Resolved

--repopen, -r reopen

Reopen strategies can only be applied to alerts with a status of Dismissed or Resolved. The following combinations of status/reopen are allowed: Dismissed -> Never; Resolved -> Never; Resolved -> SignificantUpdates

Supported values are: Never, SignificantUpdates

--priority, -P priority

Set a new alert priority

Possible values are: Informational, Moderate, High

--comment, -t comment

Comment to add to the alert(s), for example: "Bulk resolved via banshee"

--assignee, -a assignee

New user to assign the alert(s) to. Accepts uhash of the user, for example: uhash:3aXZxdkM12

--help, -h

Show help for this command

Provide one or more alert IDs (whitespace separated) and specify the desired update options:


banshee pba update ALERT_ID -s Dismissed
banshee pba update ALERT_ID -s InProgress -p High -t "Escalated due to new findings"
banshee pba update ALERT_ID_1 ALERT_ID_2 -s Resolved -a uhash:3aXZxdkM12
1. Directly as arguments (single or multiple): 

banshee pba update ALERT_ID -s Resolved
banshee pba update ALERT_ID -s Resolved
banshee pba update ALERT_ID_1 ALERT_ID_2 -s Resolved
2. From a file or standard input:

If you have a file (e.g., alerts.txt) with one alert ID per line:


ALERT_ID_1
ALERT_ID_2
ALERT_ID_3

You can update all listed alerts using:


banshee pba update -s Dismissed < alerts.txt
cat alerts.txt | banshee pba update -s Dismissed
3. By piping from a search command:

Use tools like jq to extract alert IDs from search results and pipe them into the update command:


banshee pba search | jq -r '.data[].playbook_alert_id' | banshee pba update -p High -t "Investigation started"


banshee pba search -c domain_abuse -P Informational | jq -r '.data[].playbook_alert_id' | banshee pba update -s Resolved
banshee pba update ALERT_ID -s Resolved -r Never
banshee pba update ALERT_ID_1 ALERT_ID_2 -s InProgress -p Informational -t "Bumping priority down due to recent findings."
banshee pba update ALERT_ID -a

docs.title: banshee pcap
docs.text:
Analyze packet capture (pcap) files by enriching them with Recorded Future Intelligence

Usage 
banshee pcap [OPTIONS] COMMAND [ARGS]...
Commands banshee pcap analyze

Analyze a packet capture (pcap) file

docs.title: banshee pcap analyzeUsageArgumentsOptions
docs.text:
Analyze a packet capture (pcap) file.

By default the command will print the results in JSON format.

The command does this and that and other cool things we should desribe here....

banshee pcap analyze [OPTIONS] FILE_PATH
FILE_PATH

Path to the pcap file to analyze

--interactive, -i

Display results in an interactive Terminal User Interface (TUI)

--help, -h

Show help for this command

docs.title: Environment variables
docs.text: banshee uses the following environment variables:

docs.title: RF_TOKEN
docs.text:
In order for banshee to get authorized against Recorded Future APIs an API token is needed. The user can either set the token as an environment variable, or provide it as an argument -k or --api-key to banshee directly:

banshee -k <API-TOKEN> ca search

docs.title: HTTP_PROXY
docs.text:
If your organisation requires you to use a proxy then ensure you set HTTP_PROXY, for example:

export HTTP_PROXY="http://10.10.1.10:3128"

For more information please see requests documentation

docs.title: HTTPS_PROXY
docs.text:
If your organisation requires you to use a proxy then ensure you set HTTPS_PROXY, for example:

export HTTPS_PROXY="http://10.10.1.10:1080"
For more information please see requests documentation

Tip

If using either of the proxy environment variables, you might need to disable SSL verification. This can be achieved with the -s, --no-ssl-verify flags passed to banshee directly:

$ banshee -s ca search

docs.title: Banshee use cases overview
docs.text:
Below are some of the core use cases to get started:

  • Alerts Management
  • Entity Match
  • IOC Enrichment
  • Packet Capture Analysis
  • Watchlist Management

docs.title: Use Case Summary
docs.text: Manage, triage, and bulk update Recorded Future alerts (Classic & Playbook) directly from the terminal to accelerate Security Operations Center (SOC) response and investigation workflows.

docs.title: Issue
docs.text: Switching to the UI for every alert delays investigation, resulting in analyst fatigue and inconsistent alert handling. Manual triage processes slow down incident response and create bottlenecks in security operations workflows.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
Retrieve and manage Recorded Future alerts directly from the terminal using banshee ca and banshee pba commands.

  • For Classic Alerts, use banshee ca search with time filters and banshee ca update for bulk status changes, note additions, and assignee updates.

  • For Playbook Alerts, leverage banshee pba search with category and priority filters, then use banshee pba update to modify status, add comments, assign users, and set reopen strategies.

This approach speeds up triage, maintains alert consistency, and enables analysts to update multiple alerts simultaneously through bulk operations.

docs.title: Use Case Summary
docs.text:
Enrich Indicators of Compromise (IOCs) with Recorded Future risk scores, related entities, and analyst context to accelerate Security Operations Center (SOC) triage and threat investigations.

For further information on IOC enrichment click here.

docs.title: Issue
docs.text: Analysts spend time pivoting between tools for IPs/domains/URLs/hashes/vulnerabilities, which slows investigations and increases response times. Manual correlation of threat intelligence across multiple sources creates gaps in analysis and delays incident response.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text: Enrich IOCs directly in Banshee using banshee ioc commands. Lookup individual indicators to get risk scores, AI insights, and threat actor/malware associations. Search for IOCs using various filtering options to identify high-risk indicators. Leverage the enriched context to understand what threats the organization is facing and accelerate incident response decisions.

docs.title: Use Case Summary
docs.text:
Search and resolve Recorded Future Entities (Companies, Malware, Threat Actors, etc.) to ensure consistent referencing across Security Operations Center (SOC) tools and workflows.

For further information on Recorded Future entities click here.

docs.title: Issue
docs.text: Free-text names can cause mismatches between tools and Recorded Future. A Threat Actor entity can have the same name as a Username entity, but their entity IDs will be different, leading to confusion and incorrect threat intelligence associations.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
Search and resolve entities directly in Banshee using banshee entity commands.

  • Use banshee entity search when you have an entity name and/or type and need to find the corresponding entity ID.

  • Use banshee entity lookup when you have an entity ID and need to retrieve the name and type.

Once you have the correct entity ID, utilize it in subsequent Banshee commands like banshee list add to ensure accurate entity referencing in your organization's watchlists.

docs.title: Use Case Summary
docs.text: Enrich packet capture files and observed IPs/domains with Recorded Future Intelligence to accelerate network security investigations and threat hunting activities.

docs.title: Issue
docs.text: Raw PCAPs show network traffic but lack threat context. Analysts must manually look up IPs/domains to identify risk or threat activity, which is time-consuming and prone to oversight during high-volume investigations.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text: Enrich network traffic directly in Banshee using banshee pcap commands. Use banshee pcap analyze to automatically parse packet captures, enrich observed indicators with threat intelligence, and display results directly in the terminal. Pipe enriched IOCs to banshee ioc lookup for deeper analysis or add high-risk indicators to Watch Lists for long-term tracking and monitoring.

docs.title: Use Case Summary
docs.text:
Use Recorded Future Watch Lists to maintain curated, high-priority entities (companies, domains, IPs, vulnerabilities, executives, suppliers) so alerts and intelligence focus on what matters most to the Security Operations Center (SOC).

For further information on Watch lists click here.

docs.title: Issue
docs.text: Threat environments and business priorities change constantly. Without actively maintained Watch Lists, alerts and intelligence may be too broad, making it harder for analysts to focus quickly on what matters most.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text: Create and maintain Watch Lists directly in Banshee using banshee list commands. Upload CSVs for bulk changes, add entities individually, or request curation for new ones. Map Watch Lists to Intelligence Goals so only relevant alerts are triggered. Review and refresh the lists regularly (brands, domains, executives, suppliers, vulnerabilities, locations, etc.) to ensure coverage reflects your evolving risk landscape.
